Objectives: 3.6 Research Objectives
The objectives of this study are as follows:
To implement SHAP-based explainability in unsupervised autoencoder models.
To optimize model inference time using ONNX conversion, pruning, and quantization techniques.
To evaluate performance improvements against baseline models (XGBoost, Isolation Forest)
To provide a scalable, interpretable, and low-latency fraud detection framework for financial institutions.

The significance spans academic, practical, and policy realms. Academically, it advances anomaly detection by unifying unsupervised variants with XAI and efficiency, using imbalanced metrics for reproducible insights [8]. Practically, it enables low-latency deployment, reducing false positives and analyst burden while aligning with instant payments rails [7]. Regulatorily, SHAP ensures GDPR/PSD2 compliance through transparent attributions, supporting ethical AI in finance [14] [19]. Scope limits to AE/VAE/AAE/Sparse AE on public benchmarks, excluding graphs for feasibility.
4. Research Design and Proposed Method

This study employs a quantitative, experimental research design centered on unsupervised machine learning prototyping to develop and assess an autoencoder-based anomaly detection framework for financial fraud. The approach integrates secondary data analysis with simulated real-time evaluation, avoiding qualitative methods or human subjects to prioritize computational reproducibility and empirical rigor. By focusing on controlled experiments model training, explainability addition, and performance optimization the design addresses key challenges like class imbalance and latency, adapting established methodologies for practical applicability in payment systems [34].

The proposed methods build on Neloy et al.’s autoencoder taxonomy [34], extended with SHAP for interpretability and ONNX for efficiency, executed in a Python/Keras environment. The process proceeds in structured steps, using GPU resources for training phases to emulate high-throughput conditions.
Figure 6: Conceptual Proposed Block Diagram

Inputs: Dataset named IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection Dataset will be used as input because this dataset provide a rich mix of real-world fraudulent and legitimate transaction records, making them suitable for training and evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection models.

Step 1: Data Preprocessing: This step ensure quality and reliability. Employ Pandas and Scikit-learn to clean and prepare data which involves handling missing values, addressing class imbalance between fraudulent and non-fraudulent transactions, managing outliers and inconsistencies, and normalizing features for consistency. Additionally, the data is split chronologically to replicate the real-world flow of financial transactions, allowing the models to learn temporal transaction patterns effectively.

Step 2: Model Development and Training: The model development stage focuses on training different types of auto-encoder architectures, including standard AE, sparse AE, adversarial AE, and variational AE – using TensorFlow/Keras. These models are designed to capture underlying transaction behaviors, where anomalies such as fraudulent activities manifest as high reconstruction errors. By experimenting with multiple auto-encoder variants, the framework aims to identify the most effective architecture for real-time fraud detection. Training occurs on normal data only, incorporating baselines like XGBoost and Isolation Forest for comparison. Hyperparameters are tuned via grid to balanced generalization and sparsity.

Step 3: Explainability Integration: A key contribution of the framework lies in explainability integration. While auto-encoders are powerful for unsupervised anomaly detection, they often lack interpretability. To address this, SHAP values are applied to anomaly scores, feature attributions are generated to highlight which variables most influence the detection, and visual outputs are produced for intuitive understanding. This ensures that stakeholders not only receive anomaly alerts but also gain insights into why a particular transaction is flagged. Also this step decouples explanations from the core inference path to maintain speed.

Step 4: Latency Optimization: To make the system suitable for real-time deployment, latency optimization techniques are applied. These include converting models into the ONNX format for faster execution, applying pruning and quantization to reduce model complexity, and implementing dynamic thresholding for efficient anomaly decision-making []. Such optimizations ensure that fraud detection operates with minimal delay, a crucial requirement in financial systems.

Step 5: Evaluation and Benchmarking: Finally, the framework undergoes evaluation and benchmarking using both accuracy-based and efficiency-based metrics. Metrics such as AUC-PR, Precision at 100, and F1 Score assess detection quality, while latency (p95) and throughput measure system performance under real-time constraints. The ultimate outputs of this framework are real-time anomaly scores, clear explanations for flagged transactions, optimized system latency, and improved anomaly detection performance.
5. Research Requirements Analysis and Specification

This section delineates the core hardware, software, and dataset requirements for developing and evaluating the unsupervised autoencoder framework, prioritizing accessibility and alignment with institutional capabilities. As a computational machine learning investigation, the emphasis is on tools supporting model training, explainability integration, and performance assessment, without reliance on primary data gathering. Requirements are scaled for efficient handling of benchmark datasets, informed by standard practices in fraud detection prototyping.
5.1 Hardware Specifications

Graphics Processing Unit (GPU): NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060. Justification: Accelerates training of autoencoder variants in Keras/TensorFlow, enabling efficient hyperparameter exploration on imbalanced data.
Central Processing Unit (CPU): Intel Core i7 series. Justification: manages ONNX-based inference and preprocessing, simulating real-world latency in transactional workflows [29].
Random Access Memory (RAM): 32 GB DDR4. Justification: Supports in-memory dataset loading and SHAP computations for seamless batch processing.
Storage: 1 TB NVMe SSD. Justification: Facilitates quick access during iterative experiments and model storage.
Overall setup is cost-effective, leveraging university facilities to minimize procurement needs.
5.2 Software Specifications
Programming Environment: Python via Anaconda.
Justification: Provides a robust ecosystem for ML dependencies and workflow management.
Machine Learning Framework: TensorFlow and Keras.
Justification: Essential for autoencoder construction and seamless ONNX export.
Optimization Runtime: ONNX Runtime.
Justification: Supports model compression techniques for reduced latency.
Explainability Library: SHAP.
Justification: Enables feature attributions on reconstruction outputs.
Data Processing Tools: Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn.
Justification: Handles preprocessing tasks and imbalanced evaluation metrics.
Development Inference: Jupyter Notebook with Matplotlib.
Justification: Aids in prototyping visualization and ensuring reproducibility.
All tools are open-source, readily available through standard installation methods.
5.3 Dataset Specifications
Primary Dataset: IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection (from Kaggle). Justification: Captures production-scale heterogeneity for realistic feature engineering.
6. Ethical Considerations

This research poses negligible risk, utilizing solely secondary, fully de-identified public dataset (IEEE-CIS) with no personally identifiable information, ensuring compliance with the Privacy Act 1988. No human participants are involved; preprocessing maintains anonymization through aggregation and PCA, preventing re-identification [25] [24]. An ethics application has been submitted to the Research Ethics Committee (REC) under the negligible risk category (approval anticipated by November 2025), detailing data security (encrypted local storage) and bias mitigation protocols. Outputs remain non-sensitive, aligning with GDPR anonymization principles [14]. Future iterations will incorporate human-in-the-loop for alert reviews to enhance oversight.
7. Initial Findings

This study is categorized as negligible risk, relying exclusively on secondary, de-identified public dataset such as the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection dataset from Kaggle. These sources contain no personally identifiable information (PII), with features anonymized through PCA transformation and aggregation to prevent re-identification. Preprocessing steps, including imputation and normalization, further ensure data remains aggregated and non-traceable to individuals. As no human participants are involved – no surveys, interviews, or real-time data collection, the research avoids direct ethical interactions, focusing instead on offline simulations of transactional anomalies. All computations occur on secure, local hardware with encryption (AES-256), and no data is shared externally, aligning with the Australian Privacy Act 1988.

The preliminary results of this suggest three important limitations in the existing literature. First, even though explainable AI (XAI) techniques are widely used with supervised fraud detection, there is a striking absence of methods providing interpretable, feature-level explanations for an unsupervised anomaly detection – particularly in models based on autoencoders which discriminate the normal from abnormal transactions using reconstruction errors or latent deviations. Second, although there has been substantial algorithmic development, few studies evaluate the system in a real-time manner by conducting full-fledged latency benchmarking or consider optimizing deployment pipelines such as ONNX, quantization and pruning for instant-payment systems with millisecond-level inference. Third, there is a shortage of integrated frameworks that simultaneously optimize anomaly detection accuracy, interpretability, and low-latency performance, underscoring the need for cohesive, production-ready solutions that unite explainability and computational efficiency.
For research methodology database name IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection was downloaded and try to run on python environment installing suitable libraries necessaries for the dataset.

9. Expected Outcomes

This study is expected to deliver a practical, real-time fraud detection framework built on unsupervised autoencoders that learn normal transaction patterns and quickly flag unusual behavior. It will provide clear, feature-level explanations for each alert using SHAP and achieve faster responses by optimizing models with ONNX, pruning, and quantization to meet strict sub-50 milliseconds latency targets. The approach will be rigorously evaluated on standard benchmark datasets (such as IEEE-CIS), demonstrating measurable gains in AUC-PR, f1-score, Precision@k, and inference speed compared to baseline methods. Finally, it will produce a clear adoption roadmap covering data pipelines, monitoring, human-in-the-loop review, governance and audit documentation and performance/latency SLAs to help financial institutions implement explainable and efficient AI for fraud detection.
10. Conclusion, limitations and future work

This candidature confirmation report proposes an unsupervised autoencoder framework enhanced with SHAP explainability and ONNX latency optimizations for real-time financial fraud detection, addressing class imbalance, label delays, and regulatory transparency on IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection dataset. Progress includes literature synthesis, preprocessing completion (Imbalanced preserved, no leakage), and ethics submission, yielding initial baselines.

Limitations encompass reliance on public data (lacking live noise/volumes), tabular scope excluding relational graphs, and offline simulations omitting full QPS testing, potentially underestimating production drifts. These constrain generalizability but enable focused feasibility.

Future work: Train variants and integrate optimizations; extend to PaySim for drift; hybrid explorations next semester. Achievements: methodological blueprint and exploratory yields position the study for empirical advancements and publications.
11. Plans for Future Publication

In order to disseminate the results from this thesis to a larger international academic community, I will also draft and submit an article titled “Real-Time Anomaly Detection on Financial Transactions Using ML for Fraud Prevention”. In the following sections, we summarize the main approaches and experimental results of this work, I would like to submit it on top international journals of high-impact factor, for example IEEE transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering or Expert Systems with Applications since they are known for publishing state-of-the-art research in data science, artificial intelligence, and fraud detection.

In addition to journal publications, I will disseminate the main findings of this work via top international conference such as International Conference on Emerging Technologies (ICETE 2026) which will be held on Melbourne in 9-10 February 2026. This prestigious venue will offer the possibility to share results with world-class researchers, receive constructive feedback and take part in discussions that may influence future directions of this work.

Publication and presentation in this forum will broaden the impact of this research, advance academic partnerships, and support further development in real-time anomaly detection and financial fraud defense world-wide among academia.
